submission.raw_text:
My name is Alex Rivera and I’m a freelance web designer. In early April I agreed to build a five-page marketing website for Maya Chen’s consulting business for $2,400. She paid a $1,200 deposit, with the balance due when the project was completed. The signed agreement is attached.

The intended launch was around May 20, although the contract also says the timeline depended on Maya supplying final copy and images by April 25. She did not send everything by then. I think the last major batch arrived around May 8 or May 9, but some small text changes kept coming after that.

The original job was a homepage, about page, services page, contact page, and mobile-responsive layout, with two revision rounds. During the project Maya also asked for a pricing comparison section, a newsletter signup, and several changes to the homepage design. I accepted most of those requests through WhatsApp because I wanted to keep the client happy. I probably should have documented more clearly which requests were outside the original scope.

I sent what I considered a complete staging version on June 3. Maya replied that it “looked really good overall” but gave me a list of changes. I made most of them during the following week. She later said the project was too late, that the mobile version had major problems, and that the delay had damaged a marketing campaign she planned to run.

There were some mobile issues, but I do not agree that the website was unusable. I fixed the issues I could reproduce. Maya also used images from the website in social media posts, and I believe at least part of the site was briefly published, although I cannot prove exactly what was live or for how long.

Maya now refuses to pay the remaining $1,200 and has also said she may ask for the deposit back. She says I disappeared for two weeks. That is exaggerated. I was slower for part of one week because of a family issue, but I still replied to messages and told her what was happening.

I feel like she is using the missed target date as an excuse not to pay for work she received. I am asking for the remaining $1,200. I will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials when the balance is paid. I want JuryAI to consider that much of the schedule delay came from late content, added requests, and repeated changes.

agreement.agreement_summary: Alex states that in early April Alex agreed to build a five-page marketing website for Maya Chen's consulting business for $2,400. Alex says Maya paid a $1,200 deposit and the remaining $1,200 was due upon completion. Alex also states that the intended launch was around May 20, subject to Maya providing final copy and images by April 25, and that the original scope allowed two revision rounds.

terms.person_a_interpretation: The original scope covered the named pages and mobile-responsive layout; the relationship between the stated five-page total and the four individually named pages is unclear.

It is unclear whether the added pricing comparison section, newsletter signup, and homepage changes changed the price, schedule, or revision allowance.
It is unclear whether delivery of editable source files and administrator credentials upon payment was an original contractual term.

damages_claims.causal_theory: Alex claims the project was completed or substantially completed under the agreement, Maya received the work, and the remaining contract balance became payable but remains unpaid.
damages_claims.calculation_basis: $2,400 stated contract price minus the $1,200 deposit allegedly paid equals a $1,200 outstanding balance.

required_actions: After payment of the remaining balance, Alex will transfer the final editable source files and administrator credentials to Maya.
outcomes.rationale: Alex seeks the unpaid balance for work Alex says was completed and received, while offering transfer of final editable files and administrator credentials upon payment.

clarification_questions.question: What calendar year applies to the early-April agreement, April 25 content deadline, May 8 or May 9 content delivery, May 20 intended launch, and June 3 staging delivery?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative supplies month and day information but no year, preventing assignment of calendar dates.

clarification_questions.question: What was the fifth page in the stated five-page website, or was the mobile-responsive layout intended to be counted as the fifth item?
clarification_questions.reason: Only four pages are individually named, creating material uncertainty about the original scope.

clarification_questions.question: Which pricing-section, newsletter-signup, and homepage-change requests did you accept, complete, or decline, and did you tell Maya that any would change the price, timeline, or revision count?
clarification_questions.reason: The precise added scope and its schedule or payment effect are not stated.

clarification_questions.question: Which requested changes remained unfinished after the week following June 3, if any, and what work did you consider necessary before the project was fully complete?
clarification_questions.reason: Alex says most changes were made, while payment was triggered by completion, so the remaining work is decision-critical.

clarification_questions.question: What specific mobile issues occurred, on which devices or browsers, when were they reported and repaired, and is there any staging history, screenshot, recording, or message showing the repairs?
clarification_questions.reason: The severity and remediation of the acknowledged mobile issues cannot be assessed from the narrative.

clarification_questions.question: Can you identify the social media posts and provide any URL, hosting record, screenshot, analytics, or message showing which part of the website was published and for how long?
clarification_questions.reason: Alex alleges use and brief publication but expressly cannot prove the extent or duration.

clarification_questions.question: Does the signed agreement state that editable source files and administrator credentials are transferred only after full payment, and who currently controls the live-site or hosting credentials?
clarification_questions.reason: The narrative does not establish whether the proposed payment condition was an original term or the current access situation.
